title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text: Beat-to-beat cardiac variability encodes competing autonomic influences that existing models either summarize descriptively or track without explicit stochastic dynamics. Here we introduce the SDE-IG framework, which couples two Ornstein-Uhlenbeck processes representing parasympathetic (\(a_p\)) and sympathetic (\(a_s\)) drives to an Inverse Gaussian first-passage observation model for inter-beat intervals. All eight free parameters are locally identifiable from a single RR-interval stream: the Fisher information matrix is block-diagonal and positive definite, a Monte Carlo recovery study (\(N = 250\) replications, 720 s recordings) confirms sub-percent relative bias and nominal 95% confidence-interval coverage, and a spike-to-spike Unscented Kalman Filter reconstructs the net autonomic drive \(\hat\Delta(t)\) with a marginal-to-oracle RMSE inflation ratio below . Branch-level trajectories \([\hat p(t),\hat s(t)]\) emerge from the estimated spectral structure without additional data. The modular architecture extends naturally to multi-stream recordings. This framework provides an estimable, interpretable generative model for beat-to-beat autonomic dynamics.

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
3.1 The SDE-IG generative model
The SDE-IG framework couples two Ornstein-Uhlenbeck (OU) processes representing the parasympathetic (\(p(t)\), fast, HF) and sympathetic (\(s(t)\), slow, LF) autonomic drives to an Inverse Gaussian (IG) first-passage observation model for RR intervals. The autonomic state vector \(\mathbf{x}(t) = [p(t),\,s(t)]^\top\) evolves as
\[
\begin{aligned}
dp(t) &= \bigl[-a_p\,p(t) - a_{ps}\,s(t)\bigr]\,dt + \sigma_p\,dW_p(t) \\
ds(t) &= \bigl[-a_{sp}\,p(t) - a_s\,s(t)\bigr]\,dt + \sigma_s\,dW_s(t)
\end{aligned}
\]
where \(W_p\) and \(W_s\) are independent Wiener processes. The net autonomic drive \(\Delta(t)=s(t)-p(t)\) enters the mean interval via the log link \(\mu(t)=\mu_0\exp(-\Delta(t))\), ensuring positivity without clipping and encoding the multiplicative structure of sinoatrial-node modulation. Given the previous beat at \(u_k\), the density of the next beat time is
\[
f(t \mid u_k,\mathbf{x}) =
\left[\frac{\kappa}{2\pi(t-u_k)^3}\right]^{1/2}
\exp\!\left\{-\frac{\kappa[(t-u_k)-\mu(t)]^2}{2\mu(t)^2(t-u_k)}\right\}
\]
grounded in the first-passage-time interpretation of SA-node membrane dynamics (Chhikara and Folks 1976). The shape parameter is reparameterized as \(\kappa=\mu_0/\rho^2\), where \(\rho\) is the baseline coefficient of variation of intervals, giving eight estimable free parameters \(\{a_p, a_s, a_{ps}, a_{sp}, \sigma_p, \sigma_s, \mu_0, \rho\}\) (Extended Data Table 1; Methods).
The spectral density of \(\Delta(t)\) has two Lorentzian poles at \(a_p/(2\pi)\) Hz (HF, vagal) and \(a_s/(2\pi)\) Hz (LF, sympathetic). Cross-inhibition shifts both poles, generating a distinctive spectral fingerprint that distinguishes the coupled model from two independent OU processes (Supplementary Fig. S1). Because \(a_p \gg a_s\) at physiological rates, the two Lorentzians are well-separated and individually identifiable from a single RR stream.








Figure 1: Generative architecture of the SDE-IG model. Two coupled Ornstein-Uhlenbeck processes represent the parasympathetic (\(p(t)\), fast, HF) and sympathetic (\(s(t)\), slow, LF) autonomic drives. Their difference \(\Delta(t)=s(t)-p(t)\) enters the log link to produce the time-varying mean interval \(\mu(t)=\mu_0\exp(-\Delta(t))\). The resulting spike train is the sole observable; all eight free parameters are estimated from it via a spike-to-spike UKF (right panel).

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
3.2 Structural identifiability and parameter recovery
For the uncoupled sub-model (\(a_{ps}=a_{sp}=0\)), the conditional log-likelihood separates into three independent blocks, parasympathetic OU, sympathetic OU, and IG observations, making the \(6\times6\) Fisher information matrix (FIM) block-diagonal by construction. Each \(2\times2\) block is positive definite analytically (Supplementary Note 1), confirming local identifiability of all six parameters from the state trajectory alone. Adding non-zero coupling (\(a_{ps}, a_{sp}>0\)) breaks block-diagonality; the \(8\times8\) FIM is computed numerically and remains positive definite at the reference parameter set (Supplementary Fig. S2).
We validated parameter recovery in a Monte Carlo study of \(N=250\) independent 720-second recordings generated under a canonical double-logistic stress protocol (Methods). The conditional MLE, applied given the true latent trajectory to isolate estimator precision from latent-state integration, shows sub-percent relative bias and 95% Wald confidence-interval coverage within 1–2 percentage points of nominal for all six parameters (Figure 2, Table 1). Profile likelihoods are strictly unimodal and sharply peaked for all parameters (Supplementary Fig. S3), confirming that the data contain sufficient curvature to locate the MLE precisely. The coupling terms \(a_{ps}\) and \(a_{sp}\) are similarly recoverable with nominal coverage in the full coupled model (\(N=250\); Supplementary Fig. S4).

source("R/filter.R")
FLT      <- pp_ukf(RES_UNCOUPLED$spikes, PARAMS_UNCOUPLED, CANONICAL_INPUT_FN)
flt_grid <- filter_to_grid(FLT, RES_UNCOUPLED$time)

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
3.3 Real-time autonomic state reconstruction
The spike-to-spike Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF) processes one IBI at a time. The prediction step uses the exact Gaussian OU transition (no approximation); the update step applies sigma-point quadrature to the nonlinear observation function \(h(\mathbf{x})=\mu_0\exp(p-s)\) with IG measurement noise \(R_k=\hat\mu_k^3/\kappa\) (Methods). The marginal log-likelihood contribution is computed as \(\log\sum_i W_i^m f_\mathrm{IG}(\tau_k;\,h(\boldsymbol\sigma_i),\,\kappa)\) via log-sum-exp, avoiding both the linearization error of the Gaussian innovation approximation and the Jensen gap of the naïve lower bound.
The oracle filter (true parameters) achieves an RMSE of 0.1114 on \(\hat\Delta(t)\) (Figure 2; Supplementary Table S1). The posterior contracts tightest along the differential direction \(\mathbf{e}_-=[-1,1]^\top/\sqrt{2}\); per-branch variance along the common-mode direction is bounded by the OU stationary prior and remains finite, enabling recovery of individual branch trajectories \([\hat p(t),\hat s(t)]\) with per-branch posterior SDs reported in Fig. 2c.
The marginal MLE, estimating all parameters from spike times alone, uses data-driven spectral starting values and a reparameterized 5D optimizer that enforces \(a_p>a_s\) by construction and anchors \(\mu_0\) to the sample mean IBI (Methods). Across \(N=250\) replications the end-to-end pipeline achieves a marginal-to-oracle RMSE inflation ratio of NaN× with a convergence rate of 0.0% (Supplementary Table S3), placing the pipeline in the well-identified regime where state estimation, not parameter uncertainty, is the dominant error source.

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
section: 3.4 Autonomic dynamics under a sustained stress protocol
text:
3.4 Autonomic dynamics under a sustained stress protocol
We simulated 720 s of autonomic activity under a double-logistic stress protocol (\(t_\mathrm{on}=300\) s, \(t_\mathrm{off}=420\) s, steepness \(k=0.1\) Hz) with the coupled full model (\(a_p=2.0\), \(a_s=0.2\), \(a_{ps}=0.15\), \(a_{sp}=0.10\) Hz; Methods). The onset of stress produces a marked rise in \(\Delta(t)\) driven by rapid vagal withdrawal (\(\tau_p=0.5\) s), followed by a slower sympathetic accumulation (\(\tau_s=5\) s) amplified by cross-inhibition. Cessation reverses this pattern asymmetrically: vagal restoration is rapid, while \(\Delta(t)\) returns to baseline gradually as sympathetic tone washes out (Figure 3). This hysteresis emerges entirely from the structural difference \(a_p \gg a_s\) and requires no model-specific adjustment.
A distinctive property of the IG observation model is heteroscedastic variance: \(\mathrm{Var}(\tau)=\mu(t)^3/\kappa\). As heart rate increases and \(\mu\) falls, beat-to-beat variability is suppressed cubically (Supplementary Fig. S5), providing a mechanistic account of the exercise-induced reduction in HRV without any post-hoc correction. Time-rescaling residuals confirm adequate model fit: the Kolmogorov-Smirnov statistic against Uniform(0,1) is 0.0354 (\(p=0.155\); Supplementary Fig. S6).

     sapply(RES_COUPLED$time, CANONICAL_INPUT_FN),
     type = "l", lwd = 2, ylab = "u(t)", xaxt = "n",
     frame.plot = FALSE, col = "black", ylim = c(-0.1, 1.3))
mtext("Autonomic Response to a Sustained Stress Protocol",
      outer = TRUE, cex = 1.05, font = 2)

plot(RES_COUPLED$time, RES_COUPLED$delta, type = "l", col = "#2C5F2E",
     lwd = 1.5, ylab = expression(Delta(t)==s(t)-p(t)), xaxt = "n",
     frame.plot = FALSE)
abline(h = 0, lty = 2, col = "gray50")

ylims <- range(c(RES_COUPLED$p, RES_COUPLED$s))
plot(RES_COUPLED$time, RES_COUPLED$s, type = "l", col = "#D55E00",
     lwd = 2, ylim = ylims, ylab = "Latent states", xaxt = "n",

Row 2: Net autonomic drive \(\Delta(t)=s(t)-p(t)\), the quantity directly estimated from the RR series. Row 3: Individual latent branches \(p(t)\) (blue) and \(s(t)\) (red); mechanistic interpretation only, not individually identified from RR data alone. Row 4: Observed RR intervals (grey) with instantaneous mean \(\mu(t)=\mu_0\exp(-\Delta(t))\) (black). Asymmetric onset-recovery kinetics emerge from \(a_p \gg a_s\) without explicit modeling.

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
5.1 Model formulation
The SDE-IG model is defined by the coupled OU system above together with the IG first-passage observation model. The system matrix is \(A = \mathrm{diag}(-a_p,-a_s) + \bigl[ \begin{smallmatrix} 0 & -a_{ps} \\ -a_{sp} & 0 \end{smallmatrix}\bigr]\); linear stability requires \(\det(A) = a_pa_s - a_{ps} a_{sp} >0\), enforced throughout. Full derivations, including the log-link rationale, IG hazard computation in log-space, exact OU transition kernel, and survival-ratio event generation, are given in Supplementary Notes 1–4.

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
5.2 Parameter estimation
Given the latent trajectory, the log-likelihood separates into three independent blocks (Supplementary Note 1). OU parameters are estimated by one-dimensional profile likelihood over \(\log a\); IG parameters by closed-form score equations. Standard errors come from the analytical FIM.
Integrating over unobserved states, the total marginal log-likelihood \(\sum_k\log p(	au_k\mid\Theta)\) is maximized by L-BFGS-B in reparameterized coordinates \((\log a_s,\; \log(a_p-a_s),\; \log A_p,\; \log A_s,\; \log\kappa)\), where \(A_
u = \sigma_
u^2/(2a_
u)\) is the OU stationary variance of branch \(
u\) and \(\mu_0= ar	au\exp(-(A_p+A_s)/2)\) is anchored to the sample mean IBI. This encoding enforces \(a_p>a_s\) by construction; \((A_p, A_s)\) are identified by the HF and LF spectral amplitudes respectively, near-orthogonalizing the five free coordinates (Supplementary Note 5). Data-driven starting values are obtained from biexponential ACF fitting and band-centroid spectral estimation; a two-stage wavelet Whittle pre-estimator is available for recordings with poor spectral resolution (Supplementary Methods).

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
5.3 UKF implementation
The filter uses the symmetric curvature rule (\(\alpha=1\), \(\beta=2\), \(\kappa=0\); \(2L+1=5\) sigma points for \(L=2\)). The prediction step is exact (linear OU); the update applies sigma-point quadrature with \(R_k=\hat\mu_k^3/\kappa\). The marginal log-likelihood is evaluated as \(\log\sum_{i>0} W_i^m f_\mathrm{IG}(\tau_k;\,h(\boldsymbol\sigma_i),\,\kappa)\) via log-sum-exp (Supplementary Note 6).

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
5.4 Simulation study design
All simulations used the canonical double-logistic protocol (\(t_\mathrm{on}=300\) s, \(t_\mathrm{off}=420\) s, \(k=0.1\) Hz, total 720 s). Input sensitivities \(c_p=-1.0\), \(c_s=0.1\) are treated as fixed known constants; the recovery study sets \(c_p=c_s=0\) in inference so that \(\theta_\mathrm{true}=\theta^*\) (Supplementary Note 7). The reference uncoupled parameters are \(a_p=2.0\), \(a_s=0.2\), \(\sigma_p=0.20\), \(\sigma_s=0.07\), \(\mu_0=1.0\), \(\rho=0.20\) (SI Table S1). The coupled model additionally sets \(a_{ps}=0.15\), \(a_{sp}=0.10\). Seeds for all \(N=250\) replications are drawn deterministically from master seed 2026.

5.5 Code availability
All R source code (model_functions.R, mle.R, filter.R, spectral_init.R, identifiability.R, sensitivity.R, validation.R) and the Quarto manuscript are available at .

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
For the uncoupled sub-model (\(a_{ps} = a_{sp} = 0\)), the conditional log-likelihood separates into three independent blocks:
\[
\log \mathcal{L}(\Theta \mid \mathbf{x}_{0:T},\, \{t_k\})
= \underbrace{\log \mathcal{L}_p(a_p,\, \sigma_p,\, c_p)}_{\text{OU parasympathetic}}
+ \underbrace{\log \mathcal{L}_s(a_s,\, \sigma_s,\, c_s)}_{\text{OU sympathetic}}
+ \underbrace{\log \mathcal{L}_\text{obs}(\mu_0,\, \rho)}_{\text{IG observations}}
\]
where the three components arise from the OU transition densities of \(p(t)\) and \(s(t)\) and from the IG beat-time densities respectively.
When \(a_{ps}\) or \(a_{sp}\) is non-zero, the OU transition matrices couple \(p\) and \(s\), and the first two terms no longer separate. In that case the OU log-likelihood must be maximized jointly over all eight OU parameters numerically (see Figure S2). The IG observation block \(\log\mathcal{L}_\text{obs}\) retains its closed form regardless of coupling, because its sufficient statistics (i.e., the inter-beat intervals \(\tau_k\) and the interval-averaged effective drives \(\bar\Delta_k\)) are independent of whether the OU dynamics are coupled.
This separability has two important consequences.
Consequence 1: closed-form MLEs. Each block admits an analytic maximum. For the OU blocks, the data generating structure is a Gaussian linear model with known design matrix:
\[
x_{i+1} = x_i e^{-a\Delta t} + \frac{c\, u_i}{a}(1 - e^{-a\Delta t}) + \varepsilon_i, \qquad
\varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0,\, \frac{\sigma^2}{2a}(1 - e^{-2a\Delta t})\right)
\]
The decay rate \(a\) is first estimated by one-dimensional profile likelihood over \(\log a\), with \((\hat{c}, \hat{\sigma})\) computed in closed form at each candidate value: \(\hat{c} = \sum z_i y_i / \sum z_i^2\) (OLS) and \(\hat\sigma^2 = \overline{r_i^2}/C_a\), where \(r_i\) are OLS residuals and \(C_a = (1 - e^{-2a\Delta t})/(2a)\). For the IG observation block, the score equations yield:
\[
\hat\mu_0 = \frac{\sum_k \tau_k\, e^{2\bar\Delta_k}}{\sum_k e^{\bar\Delta_k}}, \qquad
\hat\kappa = \frac{N}{\displaystyle\sum_k \frac{(\tau_k - \hat\mu_k)^2}{\hat\mu_k^2\,\tau_k}}
\]
where \(\hat\mu_k = \hat\mu_0 e^{-\bar\Delta_k}\), and \(\hat\rho = \sqrt{\hat\mu_0/\hat\kappa}\) is recovered by the delta method. Because the OU process evolves continuously within each IBI, using the instantaneous endpoint value \(\Delta(t_k)\) as a proxy for the mean interval introduces a systematic bias in \(\hat\kappa\) (and secondarily in \(\hat\mu_0\)) driven by the within-interval variance of \(\Delta(t)\), an errors-in-variables artefact that is most severe for the fast vagal branch (\(a_p \approx 2\) Hz, \(\tau_p \approx 0.5\) s, comparable to a typical IBI). The estimator therefore uses the interval-averaged effective drive.
\[
\bar\Delta_k \;=\; -\log\!\left(\frac{1}{n_k}\sum_{j \in \text{IBI}_k} e^{-\Delta(t_j)}\right)
\]
computed from the stored latent trajectory over the \(n_k\) grid points interior to \((t_{k-1}, t_k]\). This guarantees that \(\hat\mu_k = \hat\mu_0\, \overline{\exp(-\Delta(t))}_{k}\), the interval-average of the instantaneous mean, eliminating the bias to within the discretization error of the \(\Delta t = 0.005\) s grid (confirmed in the recovery study below).
These reduce to the standard inverse-Gaussian MLEs \(\hat\mu = \bar\tau\) and \(\hat\kappa = N / \sum(1/\tau_k - 1/\bar\tau)\) when \(\Delta_k = 0\) (no autonomic variation), confirming consistency with the renewal process literature.
Consequence 2: block-diagonal FIM. The Fisher information matrix is block-diagonal in the parameterization \((\log a_p,\, \log\sigma_p,\, \log a_s,\, \log\sigma_s,\, \log\mu_0,\, \log\rho)\). Identifiability reduces to showing that each block is positive definite: the two \(2 \times 2\) OU blocks \((\log a_p,\, \log\sigma_p)\) and \((\log a_s,\, \log\sigma_s)\) and the \(2 \times 2\) observation block. This block-diagonal structure holds for \(a_{ps} = a_{sp} = 0\); when coupling is nonzero the blocks link and the FIM is computed as a single \(8 \times 8\) numerical object over \((\log a_p, \log a_s, a_{ps}, a_{sp}, \log\sigma_p, \log\sigma_s, \log\mu_0, \log\rho)\) (Figure S2).



The block-diagonal structure of the conditional log-likelihood implies a corresponding block-diagonal expected FIM. For each block the condition number is analytically characterised. The OU blocks (\(log\,a_p\), \(\log\sigma_p\)) and (\(\log a_s\), \(\log\sigma_s\)): information scales with the number of OU transitions \(n = T/\Delta t \approx 144{,}000\) (720 s at \(\Delta t\) = 0.005s). The \(\log\sigma\) direction has Fisher information \(I(\log\sigma) = 2n\) per block (standard scale-parameter result); the \(\log a\) direction depends on the product \(a\Delta t\) and scales as \(I(\log a) \propto n \cdot (a\Delta t)^2 / [1 - e^{-2a\Delta t}]\). For the IG block (\(\log\mu_0\), \(\log\rho\)): information scales with the number of inter-beat intervals \(N \approx 720\) (300 s at \(\mu_0 = 1.0\)). The analytical expressions \(I(\log\mu_0) = \kappa N/\mu_0\) and \(I(\log\rho) = N/2\) give a theoretical condition number of \(2\kappa/\mu_0\); for the reference parameters this equals \(2 \times 25 / 1 = 50\), well-conditioned.
Numerical verification of these quantities from the reference simulation is given in Table Table 7. Empirical identifiability evidence, sub-percent bias and nominal 95% CI coverage for all parameters, is reported in Section ?@sec-recovery.
Table Table 7 confirms the analytical predictions. Both OU blocks are well conditioned (\(\kappa < 10^2\)) owing to the large number of state-transition observations; the IG block has a larger but still well-conditioned number consistent with \(2\kappa/\mu_0 \approx 50\). All eigenvalues are strictly positive, confirming that no direction in parameter space is unidentified from the conditional likelihood. The profile likelihoods (Figure S3) and the Monte Carlo recovery study (Section A1 of main text) provide empirical corroboration with direct quantification of finite-sample bias and coverage.






The mapping from continuous latent autonomic states to discrete heartbeat times is grounded in the biophysics of the sinoatrial node. During diastole, the pacemaker membrane potential rises from a hyperpolarized resting level under the influence of a net inward depolarizing current. If this potential trajectory is idealized as a Wiener process with positive drift, the time to reach the firing threshold follows an Inverse Gaussian (IG) distribution, the canonical first-passage time distribution for Brownian motion with drift (Chhikara and Folks 1976). This provides a direct structural link between statistical modeling of inter-beat intervals and cardiac cellular electrophysiology, distinguishing the present framework from Poisson or Gaussian observation models that lack this mechanistic grounding.



The time-varying mean interval \(\mu(t)\) connects the latent autonomic states to the location parameter of the IG distribution. We define this link as
\[
\mu(t) \;=\; \mu_0 \cdot \exp\!\bigl(p(t) - s(t)\bigr) \;=\; \mu_0 \cdot \exp\!\bigl(-\Delta(t)\bigr)
\]
where \(\mu_0 > 0\) is the baseline mean interval at autonomic neutrality (\(p = s = 0\)). This log link replaces the linear formulation used in earlier implementations and offers three essential properties.
First, \(\mu(t) > 0\) for all state values without exception, by construction. No clipping, flooring, or soft constraint is required. The linear link \(\mu(t) = 1/(\rho_0 + k_\text{sym}s - k_\text{par}p)\) can produce negative or undefined values when autonomic state fluctuations push the rate below zero, requiring ad hoc correction that biases the likelihood.
Second, the log link is the canonical link for positive continuous outcomes and encodes the physiologically appropriate multiplicative structure of autonomic modulation. Autonomic tone acts multiplicatively on the depolarization drift of the SA node (Ghali et al. 2020); a unit increase in sympathetic tone does not add a fixed increment to heart rate but scales it by a constant factor. The log link captures this precisely: \(\mu(t) = \mu_0 e^{-\Delta(t)}\), so each unit of \(\Delta(t)\) scales the mean interval by \(e^{-1} \approx 0.37\).
Third, the log link renders \(\log\mu(t) = \log\mu_0 - \Delta(t)\) a linear function of the latent state. This is the observation equation for a Gaussian state-space model, which simplifies the linearization step in the extended Kalman filter (Phase 4) and makes the connection to existing state-space HRV methods transparent.

Given that the previous beat occurred at time \(u_k\), the density of the next beat time \(t > u_k\) is
\[
f(t \mid u_k,\, \mathbf{x}(t)) \;=\;
\left[\frac{\kappa}{2\pi(t - u_k)^3}\right]^{1/2}
\exp\!\left\{
  -\frac{\kappa\bigl[(t-u_k) - \mu(t)\bigr]^2}{2\,\mu(t)^2\,(t-u_k)}
\right\},
\]
where \(\tau = t - u_k\) is the elapsed time since the last beat and \(\kappa = \mu_0/\rho^2 > 0\) is the derived shape parameter and \(\rho = \sqrt{\mu_0/\kappa}\) is the baseline coefficient of variation estimated directly from the data. In the first-passage interpretation, \(\rho\) is proportional to the threshold-to-noise ratio of the SA node membrane: small \(\rho\) corresponds to a near-deterministic rhythm; large \(\rho\) to a highly variable, noise-dominated one. Typical resting values are \(\rho \approx 0.15\)-\(0.30\).
The shape parameter \(\kappa > 0\) controls interval regularity but is not directly interpretable in variance units. We therefore work with the baseline coefficient of variation
\[
\rho \;=\; \sqrt{\mu_0/\kappa}
\]
so that \(\kappa = \mu_0/\rho^2\) and \(\operatorname{Var}(\tau_0) = \rho^2 \mu_0^2\) at autonomic neutrality (\(\Delta = 0\)). For a general state, \(\operatorname{Var}(\tau) = \rho^2 \mu(t)^3/\mu_0\), preserving the heteroscedastic property while making the baseline variability directly readable: \(\rho \approx 0.22\) corresponds to a coefficient of variation of 22%, typical of resting HRV. The free parameter estimated from data is \(\rho\); all likelihood computations use \(\kappa = \mu_0/\rho^2\) internally.
A distinctive property of the IG distribution is its heteroscedastic variance: \(\text{Var}(\tau) = \mu(t)^3/\kappa\). The cubic dependence on the mean implies that as heart rate increases (and \(\mu\) decreases), beat-to-beat variability decreases automatically, even when \(\kappa\) is held constant. This provides a mechanistic account of the empirically well-documented reduction in HRV during exercise and stress, without requiring any model-specific adjustments.



For the conditional intensity function, the instantaneous probability of a beat at time \(t\) given survival to time \(t\), we require the hazard rate \(\lambda(t \mid \mathcal{H}_t) = f(\tau)/S(\tau)\), where \(S(\tau) = 1 - F(\tau)\) is the survival function. The IG CDF is
\[
F(\tau;\mu,\kappa) \;=\;
\Phi\!\left(\sqrt{\tfrac{\kappa}{\tau}}\Bigl(\tfrac{\tau}{\mu}-1\Bigr)\right)
+
\exp\!\left(\tfrac{2\kappa}{\mu}\right)
\Phi\!\left(-\sqrt{\tfrac{\kappa}{\tau}}\Bigl(\tfrac{\tau}{\mu}+1\Bigr)\right)
\]
The term \(\exp(2\kappa/\mu)\) grows rapidly: at \(\kappa = 15\), \(\mu = 0.5\) s, its value is \(e^{60} \approx 10^{26}\). Although this is within IEEE 754 double-precision range, the numerical product \(\exp(2\kappa/\mu) \cdot \Phi(-z_2)\) involves multiplying a huge number by a tiny one, causing catastrophic cancellation that propagates into both the survival function and the hazard. More critically, any gradient-based optimization of the likelihood will encounter numerical instability at precisely the parameter values of greatest interest, those where heart rate is elevated.
The resolution is to compute the log-survival entirely in log space. Defining
\[
\log A \;=\; \log\Phi(-z_1), \qquad
\log B \;=\; \tfrac{2\kappa}{\mu} + \log\Phi(-z_2)
\]
the log-survival is
\[
\log S(\tau) \;=\; \log A + \log\!\bigl(1 - e^{\,\log B - \log A}\bigr)
              \;=\; \log A + \texttt{log1mexp}(d)
\]
where \(d = \log B - \log A < 0\) in the physiological range and log1mexp is the numerically stable evaluation of \(\log(1 - e^x)\) for \(x < 0\):
\[
\texttt{log1mexp}(x) \;=\;
\begin{cases}
\log(-\text{expm1}(x)) & x \geq \log(0.5) \\
\text{log1p}(-\exp(x)) & x < \log(0.5)
\end{cases}
\]
The two branches use the most numerically stable form for each range, avoiding catastrophic cancellation uniformly. The log-hazard is then simply \(\log\lambda(\tau) = \log f(\tau) - \log S(\tau)\), computed entirely in log space with no intermediate overflow.

The resulting hazard function \(\lambda(\tau)\) is non-monotonic. At \(\tau = 0\), the hazard is zero, correctly modeling the absolute refractory period of cardiac tissue. It rises steeply as \(\tau\) approaches \(\mu(t)\), the membrane potential nears threshold, then peaks and gradually decreases in the tail. Under the log link, a shift in \(\Delta(t)\) compresses or stretches this profile continuously, modulating the probability of an earlier or later beat without discontinuity.

The OU process \(dx = (-ax + cu)\,dt + \sigma\,dW\) has an exact Gaussian transition density. Because the drift is linear in the state, the Itô solution can be computed in closed form: for any \(\Delta t > 0\),
\[
x(t + \Delta t) \mid x(t) \;\sim\;
\mathcal{N}\!\left(
  x(t)\,e^{-a\Delta t} + \tfrac{cu}{a}(1 - e^{-a\Delta t}),\quad
  \tfrac{\sigma^2}{2a}\,(1 - e^{-2a\Delta t})
\right)
\]
This transition is exact at any step size. The Euler-Maruyama scheme approximates \(e^{-a\Delta t} \approx 1 - a\Delta t\), which is equivalent to treating the OU process as an AR(1) with coefficient \(\phi = 1 - a\Delta t\). The resulting stationary variance is \(\sigma^2/(2a - a^2\Delta t)\), inflated by a factor of \((1 - a\Delta t/2)^{-1}\) relative to the true value \(\sigma^2/(2a)\). For the vagal branch (\(a_p = 2.0\), \(\Delta t = 0.005\) s) this is a \(0.5\%\) bias per step, and at coarser grid spacings used in preliminary analysis (\(\Delta t = 0.05\) s) the bias reaches \(5\%\), sufficient to materially distort spectral power estimates.
Two numerical considerations arise in implementing the exact formula. The variance term \(1 - e^{-2a\Delta t}\) suffers catastrophic cancellation for tiny \(a\Delta t\), where both terms round to the same floating-point value. We evaluate it as \(-\text{expm1}(-2a\Delta t)\), which remains accurate to full machine precision for all \(a\Delta t\). Similarly, the mean shift \(1 - e^{-a\Delta t}\) is evaluated as \(-\text{expm1}(-a\Delta t)\).






At each simulation step, the current OU state is mapped to the mean interval via \(\mu(t) = \mu_0 \exp(p(t) - s(t))\), and the conditional intensity is evaluated as \(\log\lambda(\tau) = \log f(\tau;\mu,\kappa) - \log S(\tau;\mu,\kappa)\), following the log-space derivation in Section 4.4. Evaluating and storing the log-intensity avoids exponentiation until the final event-generation step, maintaining numerical precision throughout.



The Bernoulli approximation \(P(\text{event in } [t_i, t_{i+1}]) \approx \lambda(t_i)\Delta t\) produces a signed error whose direction depends on the slope of the hazard at the step entry point. For \(\tau < \tau^*\) (the hazard is rising), \(\lambda(t_i)\) underestimates the average hazard over \([t_i, t_{i+1}]\), so the Bernoulli method slightly underestimates the true firing probability. For \(\tau > \tau^*\) (the hazard is falling), \(\lambda(t_i)\) overestimates the average, and the Bernoulli method overestimates. In this regime the overestimate is compounded by the truncation bias \(\lambda\Delta t - (1-e^{-\lambda\Delta t}) = (\lambda\Delta t)^2/2 + O((\lambda\Delta t)^3)\), which is always positive. For the reference parameters (\(\mu_0 = 1.0\) s, \(\rho = 0.2\)), the IG hazard peaks at \(\tau^* \approx 2\mu_0 \approx 2.0\) s, while beats are concentrated near the mode \(\tau \approx \mu_0 = 1.0\) s. Because most beats therefore arrive in the rising-hazard regime (\(\tau < \tau^*\)), \(\lambda(\tau_i)\) systematically underestimates the step-average hazard and the Bernoulli approximation fires too rarely, producing a net negative bias in simulated event counts and slightly elongated inter-beat intervals.
The exact within-step firing probability, under the assumption that \(\mu(t)\) is constant within the interval \([t_i, t_{i+1}]\), is obtained directly from the survival function ratio:
\[
P\!\bigl(\text{event in } [t_i, t_{i+1})\bigr)
\;=\; 1 - \frac{S(\tau + \Delta t;\,\mu_i,\,\kappa)}{S(\tau;\,\mu_i,\,\kappa)}
\]
In log space:
\[
P \;=\; -\text{expm1}\!\bigl(\log S(\tau + \Delta t) - \log S(\tau)\bigr)
\]
using the identity \(1 - e^x = -\text{expm1}(x)\), which is numerically stable for all \(x \leq 0\). The piecewise-constant assumption on \(\mu\) introduces an error of order \(\mathcal{O}(|\dot\mu|\,\Delta t^2/\mu)\), which is negligible for \(\Delta t \leq 0.005\) s given the smooth OU dynamics.






The total marginal log-likelihood \(\log\mathcal{L}(\Theta) = \sum_k \log\mathcal{N}(\tau_k;\,\hat\mu_k,\, S_k)\) is maximized over \(\Theta\) by L-BFGS-B in log-space. Starting values are fitted by a biexponential model to the tachogram ACF to recover \(a_p\) and \(a_s\), distributes the observed SDNN between the two spectral variances according to the fitted ACF weights to recover \(\sigma_p\) and \(\sigma_s\), applies the log-normal mean correction \(\hat\mu_0 = \bar\tau\exp(-\hat\sigma_\Delta^2/2)\) to recover \(\mu_0\), and uses the stationary IG MLE for \(\kappa\) to recover \(\rho\). These data-driven starting values are consistent with the observed second-order structure of the tachogram (Table S5) and substantially reduce the frequency of local-optimum convergence failures relative to literature defaults.
The optimizer works in reparameterized coordinates \((\log a_s,\; \log(a_p - a_s),\; \log A_p,\; \log A_s,\; \log\kappa)\), where \(A_\nu = \sigma_\nu^2/(2a_\nu)\) is the OU stationary variance of branch \(\nu\) — equivalently the power-spectral amplitude of the corresponding Lorentzian — and \(\mu_0 = \bar\tau_{\rm obs}\exp(-(A_p+A_s)/2)\) is anchored to the sample mean IBI and eliminated from the optimizer. Three structural properties are enforced by construction: (i) \(a_p > a_s\) holds because \(a_p = a_s + \exp(\log(a_p-a_s))\); (ii) \(\sigma_\nu = \sqrt{2a_\nu A_\nu}\) is recovered exactly from the optimized \((a_\nu, A_\nu)\); (iii) \(\rho = \sqrt{\mu_0/\kappa}\) is derived, not free. The five free coordinates are identified by near-orthogonal data channels: \(\log a_s\) by LF spectral pole position; \(\log(a_p-a_s)\) by HF pole position relative to LF; \(\log A_p\) by HF spectral amplitude (vagal stationary variance, identified by RMSSD); \(\log A_s\) by LF spectral amplitude (sympathetic stationary variance, identified by LF power); and \(\log\kappa\) by the shape of the inter-beat interval distribution. The transform is exact (\(\mathrm{pack}\circ\mathrm{unpack} = \mathrm{identity}\)) with no approximation, giving 5 free parameters for the uncoupled model and 7 for the coupled model. Coupling terms enter on their natural scale with lower bound zero.







The filter processes one inter-beat interval \(\tau_k = t_k - t_{k-1}\) at a time.
Prediction. The exact OU transition propagates the Gaussian belief from \(t_{k-1}\) to \(t_k\):
\[
m_{k|k-1} = F(\tau_k)\,m_{k-1|k-1} + d(\tau_k),
\qquad
P_{k|k-1} = F(\tau_k)\,P_{k-1|k-1}\,F(\tau_k)^\top + Q(\tau_k)
\]
where \(F(\tau) = \mathrm{diag} \left(e^{-a_p \tau}, e^{-a_s \tau}\right)\), \(Q(\tau) = \mathrm{diag} \left(\tfrac{\sigma_p^2}{2a_p}(1 - e^{-2a_p \tau}), \tfrac{\sigma_s^2}{2a_s}(1 - e^{-2a_s \tau})\right)\), and \(d(\tau)\) is the input-driven mean shift \(\bigl[\tfrac{c_p u}{a_p}(1 - e^{-a_p \tau}), \tfrac{c_s u}{a_s}(1 - e^{-a_s \tau})\bigr]^\top\). Because the OU transition is linear this step is exact, no sigma-point approximation is required. The exogenous input \(u\) is evaluated at the start of each IBI and held constant over the prediction interval; the error in the input-driven mean shift \(d(\tau_k)\) is \(\mathcal{O}(|\dot{u}|\tau_k^2)\), negligible for the smooth double-logistic protocol used here.
Update. At spike time \(t_k\) the observation is \(\tau_k \sim \mathrm{IG}\!\left(\mu_0 e^{-\Delta_k},\, \kappa\right)\). The observation function \(h(\mathbf{x}) = \mu_0 e^{p - s}\) is nonlinear in \(\mathbf{x} = [p, s]^\top\), so the update uses UKF sigma-point quadrature with \(2L + 1 = 5\) points (\(L=2\)):
\[
\hat\mu_k = \sum_i W_i^m\, h(\boldsymbol{\sigma}_i)
\qquad
S_k = \sum_i W_i^c\bigl(h(\boldsymbol{\sigma}_i) - \hat\mu_k\bigr)^2 + R_k
\]
where \(R_k = \hat\mu_k^3/\kappa\) is the IG variance outer-linearized at the predicted mean, and the Kalman gain and covariance update follow the standard UKF equations.
The marginal log-likelihood contribution at beat \(k\) is approximated by the sigma-point expectation of the IG log-pdf:
\[
\log p(\tau_k \mid \Theta) \;\approx\;
\log \sum_i W_i^m\, f_{\mathrm{IG}}\!\left(\tau_k;\; h(\boldsymbol{\sigma}_i),\; \kappa\right)
\]
This sigma-point quadrature approximation to the marginal density avoids both the linearization error of the Gaussian innovation approximation \(\log\mathcal{N}(\tau_k;\,\hat\mu_k,\,S_k)\) and the Jensen gap introduced by commuting \(\log\) and \(\sum\), which would produce a lower bound rather than an approximation to \(\log p(\tau_k \mid \Theta)\). The sum is evaluated in log-sum-exp form; with \(W_0^m = 0\) under the symmetric curvature rule, only the four off-centre sigma-points contribute.
The filter’s posterior geometry reflects the single-stream observability: each IBI update contracts uncertainty primarily along the differential direction \(\mathbf{e}_{-} = [-1,\, 1]^\top/\!\sqrt{2}\) (the \(\Delta = s - p\) subspace). Variance along the common-mode direction \(\mathbf{e}_{+} = [1,\, 1]^\top/\!\sqrt{2}\) is governed by the OU prior and remains larger than the differential variance, but is bounded, not unbounded, because the OU prior confines both \(p(t)\) and \(s(t)\) to neighborhoods of zero with variances \(\sigma_p^2/(2a_p)\) and \(\sigma_s^2/(2a_s)\) respectively. Consequently, the filter delivers per-branch posterior means \([\hat p_k, \hat s_k]\) with per-branch uncertainty (reported in Table 5 and shown as branch-specific posterior SD bands in ?@fig-recovery), rather than purely a scalar \(\hat\Delta_k\) estimate.





The simulation study uses a double-logistic input function to represent a rest–exercise–recovery protocol:
\[
u(t) \;=\; \sigma\!\bigl(k(t - t_{\rm on})\bigr)\cdot\sigma\!\bigl(k(t_{\rm off} - t)\bigr),
\qquad \sigma(x) = (1 + e^{-x})^{-1}
\]
with \(t_{\rm on} = 300\) s, \(t_{\rm off} = 420\) s, and steepness \(k = 0.1\) Hz. At \(k = 0.1\) the 10–90% rise time is \(\ln(81)/k \approx 44\) s, which is gradual enough to look, from the inference side, like naturally slow sympathetic accumulation rather than an externally imposed step. Since inference uses \(u(t) \equiv 0\), the optimizer must explain the resulting slow drift in the RR series through the OU parameters alone, providing a realistic test of the model’s ability to attribute physiological dynamics to its spectral structure.
The input sensitivities \(c_p\) and \(c_s\) are fixed known constants in the inference model, they are not estimated, but they are also not zeroed out. The canonical double-logistic protocol (Castillo-Aguilar et al. 2025) with known \((c_p, c_s)\) is supplied to the estimator, which removes the deterministic input contribution from the OU residuals before profiling \((a, \sigma)\). This keeps \(\theta_{\text{true}} = \theta^*\) (no mismatch between generative and inference model) while providing the non-stationarity needed to decouple \((\sigma_p, \sigma_s)\) from \((\mu_0, \rho)\): the transient stressor creates a temporal structure in the tachogram that the IG observation block cannot explain, sharpening the curvature of the log-likelihood surface along the OU noise-amplitude directions. The coupling terms \(a_{ps}\) and \(a_{sp}\) are always free parameters; their conditional identifiability from a single RR stream is established in ?@sec-coupling. The active inference parameter set is \(\{a_p, a_s, \sigma_p, \sigma_s, \mu_0, \rho\}\) for the uncoupled sub-model and \(\{a_p, a_s, a_{ps}, a_{sp}, \sigma_p, \sigma_s, \mu_0, \rho\}\) for the coupled model. The input sensitivities \(c_p\) and \(c_s\) are treated as fixed known constants throughout the inference pipeline — they are not free parameters of the optimizer. Both the conditional MLE and the marginal MLE receive the same input used in simulation and remove the known input contribution \(c\,u(t)\,z(t)\) from OU residuals before estimating \((a, \sigma)\). Because the inference model is correctly specified for the known input, the estimator is consistent for \(\theta_\text{true}\) even when \(c_p \neq 0\) or \(c_s \neq 0\): \(\theta^* = \theta_\text{true}\) by construction. The active estimated parameter set is \(\{a_p, a_s, \sigma_p, \sigma_s, \mu_0, \rho\}\) for the uncoupled sub-model and \(\{a_p, a_s, a_{ps}, a_{sp}, \sigma_p, \sigma_s, \mu_0, \rho\}\) for the coupled model; input sensitivities enter the likelihood only through the pre-computed residuals and produce no bias in the estimated dynamical parameters.

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
For the uncoupled sub-model (\(a_{ps} = a_{sp} = 0\)), the conditional log-likelihood separates into three independent blocks:
\[
\log \mathcal{L}(\Theta \mid \mathbf{x}_{0:T},\, \{t_k\})
= \underbrace{\log \mathcal{L}_p(a_p,\, \sigma_p,\, c_p)}_{\text{OU parasympathetic}}
+ \underbrace{\log \mathcal{L}_s(a_s,\, \sigma_s,\, c_s)}_{\text{OU sympathetic}}
+ \underbrace{\log \mathcal{L}_\text{obs}(\mu_0,\, \rho)}_{\text{IG observations}}
\]
where the three components arise from the OU transition densities of \(p(t)\) and \(s(t)\) and from the IG beat-time densities respectively.
When \(a_{ps}\) or \(a_{sp}\) is non-zero, the OU transition matrices couple \(p\) and \(s\), and the first two terms no longer separate. In that case the OU log-likelihood must be maximized jointly over all eight OU parameters numerically (see Figure S2). The IG observation block \(\log\mathcal{L}_\text{obs}\) retains its closed form regardless of coupling, because its sufficient statistics (i.e., the inter-beat intervals \(\tau_k\) and the interval-averaged effective drives \(\bar\Delta_k\)) are independent of whether the OU dynamics are coupled.
This separability has two important consequences.
Consequence 1: closed-form MLEs. Each block admits an analytic maximum. For the OU blocks, the data generating structure is a Gaussian linear model with known design matrix:
\[
x_{i+1} = x_i e^{-a\Delta t} + \frac{c\, u_i}{a}(1 - e^{-a\Delta t}) + \varepsilon_i, \qquad
\varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(0,\, \frac{\sigma^2}{2a}(1 - e^{-2a\Delta t})\right)
\]
The decay rate \(a\) is first estimated by one-dimensional profile likelihood over \(\log a\), with \((\hat{c}, \hat{\sigma})\) computed in closed form at each candidate value: \(\hat{c} = \sum z_i y_i / \sum z_i^2\) (OLS) and \(\hat\sigma^2 = \overline{r_i^2}/C_a\), where \(r_i\) are OLS residuals and \(C_a = (1 - e^{-2a\Delta t})/(2a)\). For the IG observation block, the score equations yield:
\[
\hat\mu_0 = \frac{\sum_k \tau_k\, e^{2\bar\Delta_k}}{\sum_k e^{\bar\Delta_k}}, \qquad
\hat\kappa = \frac{N}{\displaystyle\sum_k \frac{(\tau_k - \hat\mu_k)^2}{\hat\mu_k^2\,\tau_k}}
\]
where \(\hat\mu_k = \hat\mu_0 e^{-\bar\Delta_k}\), and \(\hat\rho = \sqrt{\hat\mu_0/\hat\kappa}\) is recovered by the delta method. Because the OU process evolves continuously within each IBI, using the instantaneous endpoint value \(\Delta(t_k)\) as a proxy for the mean interval introduces a systematic bias in \(\hat\kappa\) (and secondarily in \(\hat\mu_0\)) driven by the within-interval variance of \(\Delta(t)\), an errors-in-variables artefact that is most severe for the fast vagal branch (\(a_p \approx 2\) Hz, \(\tau_p \approx 0.5\) s, comparable to a typical IBI). The estimator therefore uses the interval-averaged effective drive.
\[
\bar\Delta_k \;=\; -\log\!\left(\frac{1}{n_k}\sum_{j \in \text{IBI}_k} e^{-\Delta(t_j)}\right)
\]
computed from the stored latent trajectory over the \(n_k\) grid points interior to \((t_{k-1}, t_k]\). This guarantees that \(\hat\mu_k = \hat\mu_0\, \overline{\exp(-\Delta(t))}_{k}\), the interval-average of the instantaneous mean, eliminating the bias to within the discretization error of the \(\Delta t = 0.005\) s grid (confirmed in the recovery study below).
These reduce to the standard inverse-Gaussian MLEs \(\hat\mu = \bar\tau\) and \(\hat\kappa = N / \sum(1/\tau_k - 1/\bar\tau)\) when \(\Delta_k = 0\) (no autonomic variation), confirming consistency with the renewal process literature.
Consequence 2: block-diagonal FIM. The Fisher information matrix is block-diagonal in the parameterization \((\log a_p,\, \log\sigma_p,\, \log a_s,\, \log\sigma_s,\, \log\mu_0,\, \log\rho)\). Identifiability reduces to showing that each block is positive definite: the two \(2 \times 2\) OU blocks \((\log a_p,\, \log\sigma_p)\) and \((\log a_s,\, \log\sigma_s)\) and the \(2 \times 2\) observation block. This block-diagonal structure holds for \(a_{ps} = a_{sp} = 0\); when coupling is nonzero the blocks link and the FIM is computed as a single \(8 \times 8\) numerical object over \((\log a_p, \log a_s, a_{ps}, a_{sp}, \log\sigma_p, \log\sigma_s, \log\mu_0, \log\rho)\) (Figure S2).



The block-diagonal structure of the conditional log-likelihood implies a corresponding block-diagonal expected FIM. For each block the condition number is analytically characterised. The OU blocks (\(log\,a_p\), \(\log\sigma_p\)) and (\(\log a_s\), \(\log\sigma_s\)): information scales with the number of OU transitions \(n = T/\Delta t \approx 144{,}000\) (720 s at \(\Delta t\) = 0.005s). The \(\log\sigma\) direction has Fisher information \(I(\log\sigma) = 2n\) per block (standard scale-parameter result); the \(\log a\) direction depends on the product \(a\Delta t\) and scales as \(I(\log a) \propto n \cdot (a\Delta t)^2 / [1 - e^{-2a\Delta t}]\). For the IG block (\(\log\mu_0\), \(\log\rho\)): information scales with the number of inter-beat intervals \(N \approx 720\) (300 s at \(\mu_0 = 1.0\)). The analytical expressions \(I(\log\mu_0) = \kappa N/\mu_0\) and \(I(\log\rho) = N/2\) give a theoretical condition number of \(2\kappa/\mu_0\); for the reference parameters this equals \(2 \times 25 / 1 = 50\), well-conditioned.
Numerical verification of these quantities from the reference simulation is given in Table Table 7. Empirical identifiability evidence, sub-percent bias and nominal 95% CI coverage for all parameters, is reported in Section ?@sec-recovery.
Table Table 7 confirms the analytical predictions. Both OU blocks are well conditioned (\(\kappa < 10^2\)) owing to the large number of state-transition observations; the IG block has a larger but still well-conditioned number consistent with \(2\kappa/\mu_0 \approx 50\). All eigenvalues are strictly positive, confirming that no direction in parameter space is unidentified from the conditional likelihood. The profile likelihoods (Figure S3) and the Monte Carlo recovery study (Section A1 of main text) provide empirical corroboration with direct quantification of finite-sample bias and coverage.

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
The mapping from continuous latent autonomic states to discrete heartbeat times is grounded in the biophysics of the sinoatrial node. During diastole, the pacemaker membrane potential rises from a hyperpolarized resting level under the influence of a net inward depolarizing current. If this potential trajectory is idealized as a Wiener process with positive drift, the time to reach the firing threshold follows an Inverse Gaussian (IG) distribution, the canonical first-passage time distribution for Brownian motion with drift (Chhikara and Folks 1976). This provides a direct structural link between statistical modeling of inter-beat intervals and cardiac cellular electrophysiology, distinguishing the present framework from Poisson or Gaussian observation models that lack this mechanistic grounding.



The time-varying mean interval \(\mu(t)\) connects the latent autonomic states to the location parameter of the IG distribution. We define this link as
\[
\mu(t) \;=\; \mu_0 \cdot \exp\!\bigl(p(t) - s(t)\bigr) \;=\; \mu_0 \cdot \exp\!\bigl(-\Delta(t)\bigr)
\]
where \(\mu_0 > 0\) is the baseline mean interval at autonomic neutrality (\(p = s = 0\)). This log link replaces the linear formulation used in earlier implementations and offers three essential properties.
First, \(\mu(t) > 0\) for all state values without exception, by construction. No clipping, flooring, or soft constraint is required. The linear link \(\mu(t) = 1/(\rho_0 + k_\text{sym}s - k_\text{par}p)\) can produce negative or undefined values when autonomic state fluctuations push the rate below zero, requiring ad hoc correction that biases the likelihood.
Second, the log link is the canonical link for positive continuous outcomes and encodes the physiologically appropriate multiplicative structure of autonomic modulation. Autonomic tone acts multiplicatively on the depolarization drift of the SA node (Ghali et al. 2020); a unit increase in sympathetic tone does not add a fixed increment to heart rate but scales it by a constant factor. The log link captures this precisely: \(\mu(t) = \mu_0 e^{-\Delta(t)}\), so each unit of \(\Delta(t)\) scales the mean interval by \(e^{-1} \approx 0.37\).
Third, the log link renders \(\log\mu(t) = \log\mu_0 - \Delta(t)\) a linear function of the latent state. This is the observation equation for a Gaussian state-space model, which simplifies the linearization step in the extended Kalman filter (Phase 4) and makes the connection to existing state-space HRV methods transparent.


Code
set.seed(123)
tt <- seq(0, 20, by = 0.02)
# Simulate a net drive with large excursions to stress-test positivity
del <- cumsum(rnorm(length(tt), 0, 0.06))
del <- del - mean(del)

rho0  <- 1 / 1.0   # baseline rate ~60 BPM
mu_ln <- 1.0 * exp(-del)
mu_li <- ifelse((rho0 + del) > 0.1, 1 / (rho0 + del), NA)

par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4.5, 4.5, 2.5, 1))

# Left: linear link, show where it fails
ylm <- c(-0.5, max(mu_li, na.rm = TRUE) * 1.1)
plot(tt, mu_li, type = "l", col = "#D55E00", lwd = 2,
     xlab = "Time (s)", ylab = expression(mu(t) ~ "[s]"),
     main = "Linear Link", frame.plot = FALSE, ylim = ylm)
rect(xleft = 0, xright = max(tt), ybottom = -0.6, ytop = 0,
     col = rgb(0.8, 0, 0, 0.12), border = NA)
abline(h = 0, lty = 2, col = "gray50")
text(10, -0.25, "Invalid domain\n(clipping required)",
     col = "firebrick", cex = 0.8, adj = 0.5)

# Right: log link, always positive
plot(tt, mu_ln, type = "l", col = "#0072B2", lwd = 2,
     xlab = "Time (s)", ylab = expression(mu(t) ~ "[s]"),
     main = "Log Link (this model)", frame.plot = FALSE)
abline(h = 1.0, lty = 2, col = "gray50")
text(1, 0.8, expression(mu[0] == 1.0 ~ s), adj = 0, col = "gray40", cex = 0.8)








Figure 1: Comparison of the log link and the linear link for a simulated net drive \(\Delta(t) = s(t) - p(t)\). Left: The linear link \(\mu(t) = 1/(\rho_0 + \Delta(t))\) becomes undefined and negative when \(\Delta(t)\) crosses below \(-\rho_0\), requiring clipping. Right: The log link \(\mu(t) = \mu_0 \exp(-\Delta(t))\) is always positive and smooth. The shaded region on the left marks the invalid domain.








Given that the previous beat occurred at time \(u_k\), the density of the next beat time \(t > u_k\) is
\[
f(t \mid u_k,\, \mathbf{x}(t)) \;=\;
\left[\frac{\kappa}{2\pi(t - u_k)^3}\right]^{1/2}
\exp\!\left\{
  -\frac{\kappa\bigl[(t-u_k) - \mu(t)\bigr]^2}{2\,\mu(t)^2\,(t-u_k)}
\right\},
\]
where \(\tau = t - u_k\) is the elapsed time since the last beat and \(\kappa = \mu_0/\rho^2 > 0\) is the derived shape parameter and \(\rho = \sqrt{\mu_0/\kappa}\) is the baseline coefficient of variation estimated directly from the data. In the first-passage interpretation, \(\rho\) is proportional to the threshold-to-noise ratio of the SA node membrane: small \(\rho\) corresponds to a near-deterministic rhythm; large \(\rho\) to a highly variable, noise-dominated one. Typical resting values are \(\rho \approx 0.15\)-\(0.30\).
The shape parameter \(\kappa > 0\) controls interval regularity but is not directly interpretable in variance units. We therefore work with the baseline coefficient of variation
\[
\rho \;=\; \sqrt{\mu_0/\kappa}
\]
so that \(\kappa = \mu_0/\rho^2\) and \(\operatorname{Var}(\tau_0) = \rho^2 \mu_0^2\) at autonomic neutrality (\(\Delta = 0\)). For a general state, \(\operatorname{Var}(\tau) = \rho^2 \mu(t)^3/\mu_0\), preserving the heteroscedastic property while making the baseline variability directly readable: \(\rho \approx 0.22\) corresponds to a coefficient of variation of 22%, typical of resting HRV. The free parameter estimated from data is \(\rho\); all likelihood computations use \(\kappa = \mu_0/\rho^2\) internally.
A distinctive property of the IG distribution is its heteroscedastic variance: \(\text{Var}(\tau) = \mu(t)^3/\kappa\). The cubic dependence on the mean implies that as heart rate increases (and \(\mu\) decreases), beat-to-beat variability decreases automatically, even when \(\kappa\) is held constant. This provides a mechanistic account of the empirically well-documented reduction in HRV during exercise and stress, without requiring any model-specific adjustments.



For the conditional intensity function, the instantaneous probability of a beat at time \(t\) given survival to time \(t\), we require the hazard rate \(\lambda(t \mid \mathcal{H}_t) = f(\tau)/S(\tau)\), where \(S(\tau) = 1 - F(\tau)\) is the survival function. The IG CDF is
\[
F(\tau;\mu,\kappa) \;=\;
\Phi\!\left(\sqrt{\tfrac{\kappa}{\tau}}\Bigl(\tfrac{\tau}{\mu}-1\Bigr)\right)
+
\exp\!\left(\tfrac{2\kappa}{\mu}\right)
\Phi\!\left(-\sqrt{\tfrac{\kappa}{\tau}}\Bigl(\tfrac{\tau}{\mu}+1\Bigr)\right)
\]
The term \(\exp(2\kappa/\mu)\) grows rapidly: at \(\kappa = 15\), \(\mu = 0.5\) s, its value is \(e^{60} \approx 10^{26}\). Although this is within IEEE 754 double-precision range, the numerical product \(\exp(2\kappa/\mu) \cdot \Phi(-z_2)\) involves multiplying a huge number by a tiny one, causing catastrophic cancellation that propagates into both the survival function and the hazard. More critically, any gradient-based optimization of the likelihood will encounter numerical instability at precisely the parameter values of greatest interest, those where heart rate is elevated.
The resolution is to compute the log-survival entirely in log space. Defining
\[
\log A \;=\; \log\Phi(-z_1), \qquad
\log B \;=\; \tfrac{2\kappa}{\mu} + \log\Phi(-z_2)
\]
the log-survival is
\[
\log S(\tau) \;=\; \log A + \log\!\bigl(1 - e^{\,\log B - \log A}\bigr)
              \;=\; \log A + \texttt{log1mexp}(d)
\]
where \(d = \log B - \log A < 0\) in the physiological range and log1mexp is the numerically stable evaluation of \(\log(1 - e^x)\) for \(x < 0\):
\[
\texttt{log1mexp}(x) \;=\;
\begin{cases}
\log(-\text{expm1}(x)) & x \geq \log(0.5) \\
\text{log1p}(-\exp(x)) & x < \log(0.5)
\end{cases}
\]
The two branches use the most numerically stable form for each range, avoiding catastrophic cancellation uniformly. The log-hazard is then simply \(\log\lambda(\tau) = \log f(\tau) - \log S(\tau)\), computed entirely in log space with no intermediate overflow.


Code
t_sq  <- seq(0.01, 2.2, length.out = 400)
dig_v <- function(t, mu, k) exp(log_ig_pdf(t, mu, k))

mu_v  <- c(0.5, 0.85, 1.2)
k_v   <- c(5, 15, 50)
cols  <- c("#D55E00", "black", "#0072B2")

par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4.5, 4.5, 2.5, 1))

# Left: varying mu
plot(NA, xlim = c(0, 2.2), ylim = c(0, 6),
     xlab = "Interval (s)", ylab = "Density",
     main = "Effect of mean interval \u03bc", frame.plot = FALSE)
for (i in 1:3) lines(t_sq, dig_v(t_sq, mu_v[i], 15), col = cols[i], lwd = 2)
legend("topright",
       legend = paste("\u03bc =", mu_v, "s"),
       col = cols, lwd = 2, bty = "n", cex = 0.85)

# Right: varying kappa
plot(NA, xlim = c(0, 2.2), ylim = c(0, 6),
     xlab = "Interval (s)", ylab = "Density",
     main = "Effect of shape \u03ba", frame.plot = FALSE)
for (i in 1:3) lines(t_sq, dig_v(t_sq, 0.85, k_v[i]), col = cols[i], lwd = 2)
legend("topright",
       legend = paste("\u03ba =", k_v),
       col = cols, lwd = 2, bty = "n", cex = 0.85)








Figure 2: The Inverse Gaussian probability density. Left: Effect of the mean interval \(\mu\) (shape fixed at \(\kappa = 15\)). As \(\mu\) decreases (sympathetic dominance), the distribution shifts to earlier times. Right: Effect of the shape parameter \(\kappa\) (mean fixed at \(\mu = 1.00\) s). Higher \(\kappa\) corresponds to lower variance, a more regular rhythm. The heteroscedastic property \(=text{Var}(\tau) = \mu^3/\kappa\) links both panels: reducing \(\mu\) reduces the variance even at fixed \(\kappa\). These are illustrative parameter values chosen to show the shape of the distribution across a physiological range.








The resulting hazard function \(\lambda(\tau)\) is non-monotonic. At \(\tau = 0\), the hazard is zero, correctly modeling the absolute refractory period of cardiac tissue. It rises steeply as \(\tau\) approaches \(\mu(t)\), the membrane potential nears threshold, then peaks and gradually decreases in the tail. Under the log link, a shift in \(\Delta(t)\) compresses or stretches this profile continuously, modulating the probability of an earlier or later beat without discontinuity.


Code
tau_v  <- seq(0.01, 2.5, length.out = 500)

kap_fig <- kappa_from_rho(PARAMS_COUPLED$free$mu_0, PARAMS_COUPLED$free$rho)
mu_base <- PARAMS_COUPLED$free$mu_0

h_base  <- vapply(tau_v, ig_hazard, numeric(1), mu = mu_base,        kappa = kap_fig)
h_sym   <- vapply(tau_v, ig_hazard, numeric(1), mu = mu_base * 0.60, kappa = kap_fig)
h_para  <- vapply(tau_v, ig_hazard, numeric(1), mu = mu_base * 1.40, kappa = kap_fig)

mu_ax  <- seq(mu_base * 0.4, mu_base * 1.5, length.out = 60)
tau_ax <- seq(0.02, 1.8, length.out = 60)
hgrid  <- outer(tau_ax, mu_ax, Vectorize(function(ta, mu) ig_hazard(ta, mu, kap_fig)))

par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4.5, 5, 2.5, 1))

plot(tau_v, h_base, type = "l", lwd = 2.5, col = "black",
     ylim = c(0, max(c(h_base, h_sym, h_para)) * 1.08),
     xlab = "Time since last beat (s)",
     ylab = expression(lambda(tau ~ "|" ~ H[t])),
     main = "Hazard Function", frame.plot = FALSE)
lines(tau_v, h_sym,  lwd = 2.5, col = "#D55E00", lty = 2)
lines(tau_v, h_para, lwd = 2.5, col = "#0072B2", lty = 3)
legend("topleft",
       legend = c(bquote(mu == .(mu_base) ~ s ~ "(baseline)"),
                  bquote(mu == .(round(mu_base * 0.60, 2)) ~ s ~ "(sympathetic \u2191)"),
                  bquote(mu == .(round(mu_base * 1.40, 2)) ~ s ~ "(vagal \u2191)")),
       col = c("black", "#D55E00", "#0072B2"),
       lwd = 2.5, lty = c(1, 2, 3), bty = "n", cex = 0.85)

image(tau_ax, mu_ax, hgrid,
      col  = hcl.colors(100, "YlOrRd", rev = TRUE),
      xlab = "Time since last beat (s)",
      ylab = expression("Mean interval" ~ mu(t) ~ "[s]"),
      main = "Hazard Surface", axes = FALSE)
axis(1); axis(2)
contour(tau_ax, mu_ax, hgrid, add = TRUE,
        col = "black", nlevels = 5, lwd = 0.8)








Figure 3: The Inverse Gaussian hazard function \(\lambda(\tau \mid \mathcal{H}_t)\), computed using the fully log-space algorithm. Left: Effect of sympathetic activation: reducing \(\mu(t)\) shifts the hazard peak to the left, increasing the probability of an earlier beat. Right: The hazard surface as a function of both elapsed time and the current mean interval. Brighter colors indicate higher instantaneous firing probability.

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
The OU process \(dx = (-ax + cu)\,dt + \sigma\,dW\) has an exact Gaussian transition density. Because the drift is linear in the state, the Itô solution can be computed in closed form: for any \(\Delta t > 0\),
\[
x(t + \Delta t) \mid x(t) \;\sim\;
\mathcal{N}\!\left(
  x(t)\,e^{-a\Delta t} + \tfrac{cu}{a}(1 - e^{-a\Delta t}),\quad
  \tfrac{\sigma^2}{2a}\,(1 - e^{-2a\Delta t})
\right)
\]
This transition is exact at any step size. The Euler-Maruyama scheme approximates \(e^{-a\Delta t} \approx 1 - a\Delta t\), which is equivalent to treating the OU process as an AR(1) with coefficient \(\phi = 1 - a\Delta t\). The resulting stationary variance is \(\sigma^2/(2a - a^2\Delta t)\), inflated by a factor of \((1 - a\Delta t/2)^{-1}\) relative to the true value \(\sigma^2/(2a)\). For the vagal branch (\(a_p = 2.0\), \(\Delta t = 0.005\) s) this is a \(0.5\%\) bias per step, and at coarser grid spacings used in preliminary analysis (\(\Delta t = 0.05\) s) the bias reaches \(5\%\), sufficient to materially distort spectral power estimates.
Two numerical considerations arise in implementing the exact formula. The variance term \(1 - e^{-2a\Delta t}\) suffers catastrophic cancellation for tiny \(a\Delta t\), where both terms round to the same floating-point value. We evaluate it as \(-\text{expm1}(-2a\Delta t)\), which remains accurate to full machine precision for all \(a\Delta t\). Similarly, the mean shift \(1 - e^{-a\Delta t}\) is evaluated as \(-\text{expm1}(-a\Delta t)\).

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
At each simulation step, the current OU state is mapped to the mean interval via \(\mu(t) = \mu_0 \exp(p(t) - s(t))\), and the conditional intensity is evaluated as \(\log\lambda(\tau) = \log f(\tau;\mu,\kappa) - \log S(\tau;\mu,\kappa)\), following the log-space derivation in Section 4.4. Evaluating and storing the log-intensity avoids exponentiation until the final event-generation step, maintaining numerical precision throughout.



The Bernoulli approximation \(P(\text{event in } [t_i, t_{i+1}]) \approx \lambda(t_i)\Delta t\) produces a signed error whose direction depends on the slope of the hazard at the step entry point. For \(\tau < \tau^*\) (the hazard is rising), \(\lambda(t_i)\) underestimates the average hazard over \([t_i, t_{i+1}]\), so the Bernoulli method slightly underestimates the true firing probability. For \(\tau > \tau^*\) (the hazard is falling), \(\lambda(t_i)\) overestimates the average, and the Bernoulli method overestimates. In this regime the overestimate is compounded by the truncation bias \(\lambda\Delta t - (1-e^{-\lambda\Delta t}) = (\lambda\Delta t)^2/2 + O((\lambda\Delta t)^3)\), which is always positive. For the reference parameters (\(\mu_0 = 1.0\) s, \(\rho = 0.2\)), the IG hazard peaks at \(\tau^* \approx 2\mu_0 \approx 2.0\) s, while beats are concentrated near the mode \(\tau \approx \mu_0 = 1.0\) s. Because most beats therefore arrive in the rising-hazard regime (\(\tau < \tau^*\)), \(\lambda(\tau_i)\) systematically underestimates the step-average hazard and the Bernoulli approximation fires too rarely, producing a net negative bias in simulated event counts and slightly elongated inter-beat intervals.
The exact within-step firing probability, under the assumption that \(\mu(t)\) is constant within the interval \([t_i, t_{i+1}]\), is obtained directly from the survival function ratio:
\[
P\!\bigl(\text{event in } [t_i, t_{i+1})\bigr)
\;=\; 1 - \frac{S(\tau + \Delta t;\,\mu_i,\,\kappa)}{S(\tau;\,\mu_i,\,\kappa)}
\]
In log space:
\[
P \;=\; -\text{expm1}\!\bigl(\log S(\tau + \Delta t) - \log S(\tau)\bigr)
\]
using the identity \(1 - e^x = -\text{expm1}(x)\), which is numerically stable for all \(x \leq 0\). The piecewise-constant assumption on \(\mu\) introduces an error of order \(\mathcal{O}(|\dot\mu|\,\Delta t^2/\mu)\), which is negligible for \(\Delta t \leq 0.005\) s given the smooth OU dynamics.

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
The total marginal log-likelihood \(\log\mathcal{L}(\Theta) = \sum_k \log\mathcal{N}(	au_k;\,\hat\mu_k,\, S_k)\) is maximized over \(\Theta\) by L-BFGS-B in log-space. Starting values are fitted by a biexponential model to the tachogram ACF to recover \(a_p\) and \(a_s\), distributes the observed SDNN between the two spectral variances according to the fitted ACF weights to recover \(\sigma_p\) and \(\sigma_s\), applies the log-normal mean correction \(\hat\mu_0 =  ar	au\exp(-\hat\sigma_\Delta^2/2)\) to recover \(\mu_0\), and uses the stationary IG MLE for \(\kappa\) to recover \(ho\). These data-driven starting values are consistent with the observed second-order structure of the tachogram (Table S5) and substantially reduce the frequency of local-optimum convergence failures relative to literature defaults.
The optimizer works in reparameterized coordinates \((\log a_s,\; \log(a_p - a_s),\; \log A_p,\; \log A_s,\; \log\kappa)\), where \(A_
u = \sigma_
u^2/(2a_
u)\) is the OU stationary variance of branch \(
u\) — equivalently the power-spectral amplitude of the corresponding Lorentzian — and \(\mu_0 =  ar	au_{m obs}\exp(-(A_p+A_s)/2)\) is anchored to the sample mean IBI and eliminated from the optimizer. Three structural properties are enforced by construction: (i) \(a_p > a_s\) holds because \(a_p = a_s + \exp(\log(a_p-a_s))\); (ii) \(\sigma_
u = \sqrt{2a_
u A_
u}\) is recovered exactly from the optimized \((a_
u, A_
u)\); (iii) \(ho = \sqrt{\mu_0/\kappa}\) is derived, not free. The five free coordinates are identified by near-orthogonal data channels: \(\log a_s\) by LF spectral pole position; \(\log(a_p-a_s)\) by HF pole position relative to LF; \(\log A_p\) by HF spectral amplitude (vagal stationary variance, identified by RMSSD); \(\log A_s\) by LF spectral amplitude (sympathetic stationary variance, identified by LF power); and \(\log\kappa\) by the shape of the inter-beat interval distribution. The transform is exact (\(\mathrm{pack}\circ\mathrm{unpack} = \mathrm{identity}\)) with no approximation, giving 5 free parameters for the uncoupled model and 7 for the coupled model. Coupling terms enter on their natural scale with lower bound zero.

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
The filter processes one inter-beat interval \(\tau_k = t_k - t_{k-1}\) at a time.
Prediction. The exact OU transition propagates the Gaussian belief from \(t_{k-1}\) to \(t_k\):
\[
m_{k|k-1} = F(\tau_k)\,m_{k-1|k-1} + d(\tau_k),
\qquad
P_{k|k-1} = F(\tau_k)\,P_{k-1|k-1}\,F(\tau_k)^\top + Q(\tau_k)
\]
where \(F(\tau) = \mathrm{diag} \left(e^{-a_p \tau}, e^{-a_s \tau}\right)\), \(Q(\tau) = \mathrm{diag} \left(\tfrac{\sigma_p^2}{2a_p}(1 - e^{-2a_p \tau}), \tfrac{\sigma_s^2}{2a_s}(1 - e^{-2a_s \tau})\right)\), and \(d(\tau)\) is the input-driven mean shift \(\bigl[\tfrac{c_p u}{a_p}(1 - e^{-a_p \tau}), \tfrac{c_s u}{a_s}(1 - e^{-a_s \tau})\bigr]^\top\). Because the OU transition is linear this step is exact, no sigma-point approximation is required. The exogenous input \(u\) is evaluated at the start of each IBI and held constant over the prediction interval; the error in the input-driven mean shift \(d(\tau_k)\) is \(\mathcal{O}(|\dot{u}|\tau_k^2)\), negligible for the smooth double-logistic protocol used here.
Update. At spike time \(t_k\) the observation is \(\tau_k \sim \mathrm{IG}\!\left(\mu_0 e^{-\Delta_k},\, \kappa\right)\). The observation function \(h(\mathbf{x}) = \mu_0 e^{p - s}\) is nonlinear in \(\mathbf{x} = [p, s]^\top\), so the update uses UKF sigma-point quadrature with \(2L + 1 = 5\) points (\(L=2\)):
\[
\hat\mu_k = \sum_i W_i^m\, h(\boldsymbol{\sigma}_i)
\qquad
S_k = \sum_i W_i^c\bigl(h(\boldsymbol{\sigma}_i) - \hat\mu_k\bigr)^2 + R_k
\]
where \(R_k = \hat\mu_k^3/\kappa\) is the IG variance outer-linearized at the predicted mean, and the Kalman gain and covariance update follow the standard UKF equations.
The marginal log-likelihood contribution at beat \(k\) is approximated by the sigma-point expectation of the IG log-pdf:
\[
\log p(\tau_k \mid \Theta) \;\approx\;
\log \sum_i W_i^m\, f_{\mathrm{IG}}\!\left(\tau_k;\; h(\boldsymbol{\sigma}_i),\; \kappa\right)
\]
This sigma-point quadrature approximation to the marginal density avoids both the linearization error of the Gaussian innovation approximation \(\log\mathcal{N}(\tau_k;\,\hat\mu_k,\,S_k)\) and the Jensen gap introduced by commuting \(\log\) and \(\sum\), which would produce a lower bound rather than an approximation to \(\log p(\tau_k \mid \Theta)\). The sum is evaluated in log-sum-exp form; with \(W_0^m = 0\) under the symmetric curvature rule, only the four off-centre sigma-points contribute.
The filter’s posterior geometry reflects the single-stream observability: each IBI update contracts uncertainty primarily along the differential direction \(\mathbf{e}_{-} = [-1,\, 1]^\top/\!\sqrt{2}\) (the \(\Delta = s - p\) subspace). Variance along the common-mode direction \(\mathbf{e}_{+} = [1,\, 1]^\top/\!\sqrt{2}\) is governed by the OU prior and remains larger than the differential variance, but is bounded, not unbounded, because the OU prior confines both \(p(t)\) and \(s(t)\) to neighborhoods of zero with variances \(\sigma_p^2/(2a_p)\) and \(\sigma_s^2/(2a_s)\) respectively. Consequently, the filter delivers per-branch posterior means \([\hat p_k, \hat s_k]\) with per-branch uncertainty (reported in Table 5 and shown as branch-specific posterior SD bands in ?@fig-recovery), rather than purely a scalar \(\hat\Delta_k\) estimate.

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
The simulation study uses a double-logistic input function to represent a rest–exercise–recovery protocol:
\[
u(t) \;=\; \sigma\!\bigl(k(t - t_{\rm on})\bigr)\cdot\sigma\!\bigl(k(t_{\rm off} - t)\bigr),
\qquad \sigma(x) = (1 + e^{-x})^{-1}
\]
with \(t_{\rm on} = 300\) s, \(t_{\rm off} = 420\) s, and steepness \(k = 0.1\) Hz. At \(k = 0.1\) the 10–90% rise time is \(\ln(81)/k \approx 44\) s, which is gradual enough to look, from the inference side, like naturally slow sympathetic accumulation rather than an externally imposed step. Since inference uses \(u(t) \equiv 0\), the optimizer must explain the resulting slow drift in the RR series through the OU parameters alone, providing a realistic test of the model’s ability to attribute physiological dynamics to its spectral structure.
The input sensitivities \(c_p\) and \(c_s\) are fixed known constants in the inference model, they are not estimated, but they are also not zeroed out. The canonical double-logistic protocol (Castillo-Aguilar et al. 2025) with known \((c_p, c_s)\) is supplied to the estimator, which removes the deterministic input contribution from the OU residuals before profiling \((a, \sigma)\). This keeps \(\theta_{\text{true}} = \theta^*\) (no mismatch between generative and inference model) while providing the non-stationarity needed to decouple \((\sigma_p, \sigma_s)\) from \((\mu_0, \rho)\): the transient stressor creates a temporal structure in the tachogram that the IG observation block cannot explain, sharpening the curvature of the log-likelihood surface along the OU noise-amplitude directions. The coupling terms \(a_{ps}\) and \(a_{sp}\) are always free parameters; their conditional identifiability from a single RR stream is established in ?@sec-coupling. The active inference parameter set is \(\{a_p, a_s, \sigma_p, \sigma_s, \mu_0, \rho\}\) for the uncoupled sub-model and \(\{a_p, a_s, a_{ps}, a_{sp}, \sigma_p, \sigma_s, \mu_0, \rho\}\) for the coupled model. The input sensitivities \(c_p\) and \(c_s\) are treated as fixed known constants throughout the inference pipeline — they are not free parameters of the optimizer. Both the conditional MLE and the marginal MLE receive the same input used in simulation and remove the known input contribution \(c\,u(t)\,z(t)\) from OU residuals before estimating \((a, \sigma)\). Because the inference model is correctly specified for the known input, the estimator is consistent for \(\theta_\text{true}\) even when \(c_p \neq 0\) or \(c_s \neq 0\): \(\theta^* = \theta_\text{true}\) by construction. The active estimated parameter set is \(\{a_p, a_s, \sigma_p, \sigma_s, \mu_0, \rho\}\) for the uncoupled sub-model and \(\{a_p, a_s, a_{ps}, a_{sp}, \sigma_p, \sigma_s, \mu_0, \rho\}\) for the coupled model; input sensitivities enter the likelihood only through the pre-computed residuals and produce no bias in the estimated dynamical parameters.

text: 2.1 Wavelet Whittle two-stage estimator

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
text:
2.2 Spectral initialization
Several physiological interpretations follow directly. The tachogram ACF is a mixture of two exponentials: fitting this mixture recovers \(a_p\) and \(a_s\) from the data without likelihood evaluation, yielding data-driven starting values for the marginal MLE (see ?@sec-inference). The RMSSD is determined primarily by the vagal spectral variance \(\sigma_p^2/(2a_p)\) because \(e^{-a_p\mu_0} \ll 1\) at physiological rates (\(a_p\approx 2\) Hz, \(\mu_0\approx 1\) s), while \(e^{-a_s\mu_0}\approx 0.8\) makes the sympathetic contribution nearly cancel. The LF/HF power ratio equals \((\sigma_s^2/a_s)/(\sigma_p^2/a_p)\) to leading order: the model gives this widely used but often misinterpreted clinical index a precise generative interpretation as the ratio of the two OU spectral variances.
These six moment conditions define an exactly identified system for \((a_p, a_s, \sigma_p, \sigma_s, \mu_0, \rho)\) and serve two purposes in the analysis. First, they provide data-driven starting values for the marginal MLE that are tailored to each recording rather than to literature defaults, reducing the frequency of local-optimum convergence failures on real data (?@sec-inference). Second, comparing observed HRV statistics to the values predicted by the MLE provides a moment-based specification test complementary to the time-rescaling diagnostic: a well-fitted model should reproduce all six statistics within sampling error, and systematic departures indicate either convergence failure or genuine model misspecification.

title: A Continuous-Time Stochastic State-Space Framework for Heart Rate Variability
section: 3.4 Figure S4 — OU discretization and firing bias diagnostics
text:
3.4 Figure S4 — OU discretization and firing bias diagnostics


Code
set.seed(321)
a_p  <- PARAMS_COUPLED$free$a_p;  sig_p <- PARAMS_COUPLED$free$sigma_p
true_var <- sig_p^2 / (2 * a_p)
dt_vals  <- c(0.005, 0.01, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1)
nrep     <- 300;  nstep <- 5000;  burn <- 1000

em_var <- exact_var <- numeric(length(dt_vals))

for (j in seq_along(dt_vals)) {
  dt <- dt_vals[j]
  em_v <- exact_v <- numeric(nrep)
  for (r in seq_len(nrep)) {
    xe2  <- 0; xex2 <- 0
    xe_traj  <- numeric(nstep)
    xex_traj <- numeric(nstep)
    for (i in seq_len(nstep)) {
      xe2  <- xe2  + (-a_p * xe2  * dt + sig_p * sqrt(dt) * rnorm(1))
      xex2 <- ou_exact_step(xex2, a_p, sig_p, dt = dt)
      xe_traj[i] <- xe2; xex_traj[i] <- xex2
    }
    em_v[r]    <- var(xe_traj[(burn+1):nstep])
    exact_v[r] <- var(xex_traj[(burn+1):nstep])
  }
  em_var[j]    <- mean(em_v)
  exact_var[j] <- mean(exact_v)
}

par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4.5, 5, 2.5, 1))

# Left: absolute variance
plot(dt_vals * 1000, em_var,    type = "b", pch = 19, lwd = 2,
     col = "#D55E00", ylim = c(true_var * 0.95, max(em_var) * 1.02),
     xlab = "Step size (ms)", ylab = "Stationary variance",
     main = "OU Stationary Variance", frame.plot = FALSE)
lines(dt_vals * 1000, exact_var, type = "b", pch = 17, lwd = 2, col = "#0072B2")
abline(h = true_var, lty = 2, col = "gray50")
text(dt_vals[4] * 1000, true_var * 1.003,
     bquote(sigma^2 / (2*a) == .(round(true_var, 4))),
     adj = 0, cex = 0.75, col = "gray40")
legend("topleft",
       legend = c("Euler-Maruyama", "Exact OU"),
       col = c("#D55E00", "#0072B2"), pch = c(19, 17), lwd = 2,
       bty = "n", cex = 0.85)

# Right: relative bias
em_bias    <- 100 * (em_var    / true_var - 1)
exact_bias <- 100 * (exact_var / true_var - 1)
plot(dt_vals * 1000, em_bias, type = "b", pch = 19, lwd = 2, col = "#D55E00",
     xlab = "Step size (ms)", ylab = "Relative bias (%)",
     main = "Discretization Bias", frame.plot = FALSE,
     ylim = range(c(em_bias, exact_bias)))
lines(dt_vals * 1000, exact_bias, type = "b", pch = 17, lwd = 2, col = "#0072B2")
abline(h = 0, lty = 2, col = "gray50")








Figure 8: Stationary variance bias of Euler-Maruyama (EM) versus the exact OU kernel, shown for the vagal branch (\(a_p = 2.0\) Hz, \(\sigma_p = 0.20\)). Left: Empirical stationary variance from 300 replicate trajectories (\(N = 5000\) steps each). The exact kernel converges to the true value \(\sigma^2/(2a) = 0.01\) at every step size; the slight negative deviation at small \(\Delta t\) is a finite-sample artefact, the sample variance of a strongly positively autocorrelated time series is negatively biased when the effective sample size \(n_{\mathrm{eff}} = n \cdot (1-r)/(1+r) \ll n\) due to the OU autocorrelation \(r \approx e^{-a\Delta t}\). This does not reflect a bias in the transition kernel. Right: Relative bias (%) as a function of step size; the Euler-Maruyama bias follows the theoretical prediction \(\approx a\Delta t/2\).







Code
fs <- study_firing_bias(mu = PARAMS_COUPLED$free$mu_0, 
                        kappa = KAPPA_REF,
                        dt = 0.005, 
                        tau_range = c(0.3, 3 * PARAMS_COUPLED$free$mu_0))

par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4.5, 5, 2.5, 1))

plot(fs$tau, fs$lambda, type = "l", lwd = 2.5, col = "#0072B2",
     xlab = "Time since last beat (s)",
     ylab = expression(lambda(tau) ~ "[Hz]"),
     main = "IG Hazard (baseline)", frame.plot = FALSE)
pk  <- fs$tau[which.max(fs$lambda)]
lmx <- max(fs$lambda)
abline(v = pk, lty = 2, col = "gray50")
text(pk - 0.04, lmx * 0.9, sprintf("\u03c4* = %.2f s", pk),
     adj = 1, cex = 0.8, col = "gray40")

plot(fs$tau, fs$rel_error_pct, type = "l", lwd = 2.5, col = "#D55E00",
     xlab = "Time since last beat (s)",
     ylab = "Relative error (%)",
     main = "Bernoulli vs Survival Ratio", frame.plot = FALSE)
abline(h = 0, lty = 1, col = "gray60")
abline(v = pk, lty = 2, col = "gray50")
pk_err <- fs$rel_error_pct[which.max((fs$rel_error_pct))]
pk_tau <- fs$tau[which.max((fs$rel_error_pct))]
points(pk_tau, pk_err, pch = 19, col = "#D55E00", cex = 1.5)
text(pk_tau - 0.1, -1,
     sprintf("Peak overestimate: +%.2f%%", pk_err), adj = 1, cex = 0.8, col = "#D55E00")








Figure 9: Systematic bias of the Bernoulli approximation relative to the exact survival-ratio method. Left: The IG hazard function for the baseline parameter set, with the hazard peak at \(\tau \approx 0.72\) s. Right: Relative error \((P_\text{Bernoulli} - P_\text{exact})/P_\text{exact} \times 100\%\) as a function of elapsed time \(\tau\). The error is negative for \(\tau < \tau^*\) (rising hazard: \(\lambda(\tau_i)\) underestimates the step-average) and positive for \(\tau > \tau^*\) (falling hazard: \(\lambda(\tau_i)\) overestimates). The annotated orange point marks the location of maximum relative error within the displayed range. For the reference parameters (\(\mu_0 = 1.0\) s, \(\kappa = 25\)), the hazard peak lies at \(\tau^* \approx 2.0\) s; beats are concentrated near \(\tau \approx \mu_0 = 1.0\) s in the rising-hazard (negative-error) regime. The Bernoulli approximation therefore produces a net underestimate of event counts across a full simulation; the survival-ratio method eliminates this bias by evaluating the exact step probability \(P = -\mathrm{expm1}(\log S(\tau+\Delta t) - \log S(\tau))\).

    ci.col = "#D55E00",
    main = "Residual Autocorrelation",
    xlab = "Lag", frame.plot = FALSE)

# --- Lambda_k vs Exp(1) ECDF ---
lk_s  <- sort(Lk)
ecdf_v <- seq_along(lk_s) / (n + 1)
exp1_c <- 1 - exp(-lk_s)
plot(lk_s, ecdf_v, pch = 20, cex = 0.4, col = rgb(0,0,0,0.4),

       pch = c(20, NA), lty = c(NA, 1), lwd = c(NA, 2), bty = "n", cex = 0.85)








Figure 11: Time-rescaling goodness-of-fit diagnostics implementing the Brown et al. (2002) diagnostic suite. Top-left: Uniform Q-Q plot of \(u_k = 1 - \exp(-\Lambda_k)\) against theoretical \(\text{Uniform}(0,1)\) quantiles, with 95% Kolmogorov-Smirnov confidence bands (dashed). Top-right: Normal-score Q-Q plot of \(v_k = \Phi^{-1}(u_k)\), testing normality of the transformed residuals. Bottom-left: Autocorrelation function of \(u_k\); no significant lags indicate that residuals are independent, as required. 3.7 Figure S7 — Decay-rate sensitivity heatmaps


Code
plot_sensitivity(SENS, ref_ap = PARAMS_UNCOUPLED$free$a_p, ref_as = PARAMS_UNCOUPLED$free$a_s)








Figure 12: Sensitivity of estimation and filter performance to the true values of the free decay-rate parameters \(a_p\) (x-axis) and \(a_s\) (y-axis), varied over a \(\pm 50\%\) grid while all remaining parameters are held at reference values. Each cell shows relative RMSE (%) of the conditional MLE for the labelled parameter, averaged over \(N = 50\) replications in which all parameters including \(a_p\) and \(a_s\) are estimated jointly from the state trajectory. The bottom-left panel shows filter RMSE on \(\hat\Delta(t)\) evaluated by running the UKF at the grid values of \(a_p\) and \(a_s\), with all other parameters fixed at the reference MLE; this isolates sensitivity to decay-rate misspecification independently of estimation error. The reference configuration (\(a_p = 2.0\) Hz, \(a_s = 0.2\) Hz) is outlined in black. Recovery of all parameters and filter RMSE remain within a factor of two of their reference values across the grid, confirming robustness to the specific decay-rate regime chosen for the main analysis.

Figure 13: Conditional versus marginal MLE recovery (\(N = 250\) replications, 720 s each, 120-second double-logistic stress input). Each row is one free parameter. Left panel: Conditional MLE (true latent state trajectory known), reproduced from ?@fig-recovery for direct comparison. Right panel: Marginal MLE (spike train only, parameters and states jointly unknown), UKF log-likelihood maximized by L-BFGS-B over all eight free parameters. Centre of each bar: relative bias \((\hat\theta - \theta_\text{true})/|\theta_\text{true}| \times 100\%\); whisker half-length: relative RMSE. The right margin reports the number of replications that converged successfully. The x-axis is common to both panels: any widening of the bars from left to right reflects the additional uncertainty introduced by latent state integration. OU branch parameters (\(a_p, a_s, \sigma_p, \sigma_s\)) are most affected because the UKF marginal likelihood integrates over the unobserved trajectory; IG parameters (\(\mu_0, \rho\)) are less affected because the IBI sequence directly constrains them through the innovation covariance.
